core_concept.principle: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons, resulting in different mass numbers but identical chemical properties.
core_concept.explanation: While all atoms of an element have the same number of protons, they can have different numbers of neutrons. These variations are called isotopes. The atomic mass shown on the periodic table is a weighted average of all naturally occurring isotopes, which is why it is usually a decimal number rather than a whole number.

image.alt: Diagram showing three isotopes of carbon with different neutron counts
image.caption: Isotopes have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

sections.introduction: Isotopes are variations of the same element that differ only in their neutron count. Despite having different masses, isotopes of an element behave identically in chemical reactions.

introduction_image.alt: Side-by-side comparison of hydrogen isotopes showing proton and neutron differences
introduction_image.caption: Hydrogen has three naturally occurring isotopes: protium, deuterium, and tritium.

key_concept.definition: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same atomic number) that have different numbers of neutrons, resulting in different mass numbers.
key_concept.context: Isotopes have identical chemical properties because chemistry depends on electron configuration, which is determined by proton count. However, they may have different nuclear properties, such as stability and radioactivity.

image.alt: Diagram showing same protons, different neutrons in isotopes
image.caption: Same element = same protons; different isotopes = different neutrons.

sections.introduction: Isotopes are identified by their element name or symbol followed by their mass number, which distinguishes them from other isotopes of the same element.

classifications.value: Element name or symbol followed by hyphen and mass number
characteristics: Most common in spoken language and general writing
Atomic number is implied by element name
Widely used in medicine, industry, and research
classifications.behavior: This notation clearly identifies the specific isotope while keeping the element identity obvious.

examples.explanation: Carbon-12, carbon-13, and carbon-14 are three isotopes of carbon. All have 6 protons, but have 6, 7, and 8 neutrons respectively.

image.alt: Three carbon isotopes with labeled neutron counts
image.caption: Carbon-12, carbon-13, and carbon-14 differ only in neutron count.

examples.explanation: Uranium-235 and uranium-238 are both uranium (92 protons) but U-235 has 143 neutrons while U-238 has 146 neutrons.

image.alt: Uranium-235 and uranium-238 comparison
image.caption: Different uranium isotopes have different nuclear properties.

classifications.value: Element symbol with mass number as superscript and atomic number as subscript
characteristics: Format: ^A_Z X where A = mass number, Z = atomic number
Provides complete particle information at a glance
Used in nuclear equations and detailed calculations
classifications.behavior: This notation allows quick calculation of protons (Z), neutrons (A-Z), and electrons (Z for neutral atoms).

examples.explanation: ^35_17 Cl and ^37_17 Cl represent the two stable chlorine isotopes. Both have 17 protons, but 18 and 20 neutrons respectively.

image.alt: Nuclear symbols for chlorine-35 and chlorine-37
image.caption: Nuclear notation shows both atomic and mass numbers.

sections.introduction: Most elements exist in nature as mixtures of isotopes. The relative abundance of each isotope determines the element's average atomic mass.

principles.statement: Natural abundance is the percentage of each isotope found in a naturally occurring sample of an element.
principles.application: Abundance values are determined through mass spectrometry and are used to calculate average atomic mass. Some elements have one dominant isotope; others have several isotopes in significant amounts.
principles.analogy: Like a bag of mixed coins: the average value depends on how many of each type are present, not just the individual values.
principles.example: Chlorine is 75.8% chlorine-35 and 24.2% chlorine-37, giving an average atomic mass of 35.45 amu.

image.alt: Pie chart showing chlorine isotope abundances
image.caption: Natural abundance determines the weighted average atomic mass.

principles.statement: Some isotopes are stable and persist indefinitely; others are radioactive and decay over time.
principles.application: Stable isotopes are used in tracing and analysis; radioactive isotopes are used in medicine, dating, and energy production. The ratio of stable to unstable isotopes varies by element.
principles.analogy: Like buildings: some are structurally sound (stable) and last forever; others have weaknesses (unstable) and eventually collapse (decay).
principles.example: Carbon-12 and carbon-13 are stable; carbon-14 is radioactive with a half-life of 5,730 years, useful for radiocarbon dating.

image.alt: Comparison of stable and radioactive isotopes
image.caption: Stable isotopes persist; radioactive isotopes decay over time.

sections.introduction: The atomic mass shown on the periodic table is a weighted average of all naturally occurring isotopes, calculated using their masses and relative abundances.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Atomic Mass of Chlorine
worked_examples.problem: Chlorine has two naturally occurring isotopes: chlorine-35 (mass = 34.969 amu, abundance = 75.8%) and chlorine-37 (mass = 36.966 amu, abundance = 24.2%). Calculate the average atomic mass of chlorine.

step_by_step.explanation: Convert percentage abundances to decimal form by dividing by 100.
Multiply each isotope's mass by its decimal abundance to find its contribution to the average.
Add the contributions from all isotopes to get the weighted average atomic mass.
step_by_step.calculation: Average atomic mass = 26.506 amu + 8.946 amu = 35.452 amu

step_by_step.explanation: Round to appropriate significant figures and compare to periodic table value.

worked_examples.final_answer: The average atomic mass of chlorine is 35.45 amu.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Average atomic mass = Σ(isotope mass × fractional abundance). This weighted average explains why atomic masses on the periodic table are usually decimals.

image.alt: Step-by-step calculation of chlorine atomic mass
image.caption: Weighted average accounts for both mass and abundance of each isotope.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Atomic Mass of Magnesium
worked_examples.problem: Magnesium has three naturally occurring isotopes: Mg-24 (23.985 amu, 78.99%), Mg-25 (24.986 amu, 10.00%), and Mg-26 (25.983 amu, 11.01%). Calculate the average atomic mass.

step_by_step.explanation: Convert all percentage abundances to decimal form.
Calculate the contribution of each isotope.
Sum all contributions to find the average atomic mass.

worked_examples.final_answer: The average atomic mass of magnesium is 24.31 amu.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Elements with multiple isotopes require summing contributions from all naturally occurring forms to get the correct average atomic mass.

image.alt: Magnesium isotope abundance and mass calculation
image.caption: Three isotopes contribute to magnesium's average atomic mass.

sections.introduction: Students often confuse mass number (a whole number for a specific isotope) with atomic mass (a weighted average decimal). Understanding the distinction is essential for accurate calculations.

categories.description: Mass number is the total count of protons and neutrons in a specific atom or isotope.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Always a whole number because you cannot have a fraction of a proton or neutron. Applies to individual atoms or pure isotopic samples. Used in nuclear notation and isotope identification.

examples.explanation: Mass number = 12 (6 protons + 6 neutrons). This is exact for every carbon-12 atom.

image.alt: Carbon-12 nucleus showing 6 protons and 6 neutrons
image.caption: Mass number is always a whole number for specific isotopes.

examples.explanation: Mass number = 235 (92 protons + 143 neutrons). Used to identify this specific fissile isotope.

image.alt: Uranium-235 nucleus diagram
image.caption: Mass number identifies specific isotopes for nuclear applications.

categories.description: Atomic mass is the weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Usually a decimal number because it reflects the average of different isotope masses weighted by their natural abundances. Appears on the periodic table. Used for stoichiometric calculations with natural element samples.

examples.explanation: Periodic table shows 12.01 amu, not 12.00, because carbon-13 (about 1.1% abundant) slightly increases the average above carbon-12's mass.

image.alt: Periodic table entry for carbon showing 12.01
image.caption: Atomic mass is a weighted average, usually a decimal.

examples.explanation: Periodic table shows 35.45 amu, between Cl-35 and Cl-37, reflecting the 75.8% : 24.2% abundance ratio.

image.alt: Periodic table entry for chlorine showing 35.45
image.caption: Atomic mass reflects natural isotope distribution.

sections.introduction: Different isotopes have unique properties that make them valuable for specific applications in medicine, industry, archaeology, and energy production.

process_types.function: Radioactive isotopes are used for imaging, diagnosis, and targeted cancer therapy

primary_example.explanation: Tc-99m is used in over 80% of nuclear medicine procedures. It emits gamma rays detectable by cameras, has a 6-hour half-life (minimizing exposure), and binds to various compounds to target different organs.

image.alt: Medical scan using technetium-99m tracer
image.caption: Tc-99m enables non-invasive imaging of organ function.

examples.explanation: I-131 concentrates in the thyroid gland; beta radiation destroys overactive thyroid tissue or thyroid cancer cells.
Fluorine-18 labeled glucose tracks metabolic activity; cancer cells show higher uptake due to increased metabolism.

process_types.function: Radioactive decay rates enable determination of ages for archaeological and geological samples
goals: Known half-lives provide accurate time measurement
Different isotopes cover different time ranges
Ratio of parent to daughter isotopes indicates age

primary_example.explanation: Living organisms maintain constant C-14/C-12 ratio through metabolism. After death, C-14 decays (half-life 5,730 years) while C-12 remains stable. Measuring the remaining C-14 ratio determines age up to about 50,000 years.

image.alt: Archaeological sample being dated with carbon-14
image.caption: Carbon-14 dating determines ages of organic materials.

examples.explanation: U-238 decays to Pb-206 over 4.5 billion years; used to date Earth's oldest rocks and meteorites.
K-40 decays to Ar-40 over 1.3 billion years; used to date volcanic rocks and early human fossils.

process_types.function: Specific isotopes undergo fission or fusion to release large amounts of energy
goals: Fuel enrichment increases fissile isotope concentration

primary_example.explanation: U-235 nuclei split when struck by neutrons, releasing energy and more neutrons. Natural uranium is only 0.7% U-235; enrichment to 3-5% enables sustained chain reactions in power plants.

image.alt: Nuclear fission chain reaction diagram
image.caption: U-235 fission releases energy for electricity generation.

examples.explanation: U-238 captures neutrons to form Pu-239, which is also fissile and used in some reactors and weapons.
Hydrogen isotopes fuse to form helium, releasing enormous energy; potential future clean energy source.

process_types.function: Isotopes track material flow, detect leaks, and study chemical processes
goals: Isotope ratios reveal sources and processes

primary_example.explanation: Small amounts of radioactive isotopes are injected into pipelines. Detectors along the route identify leaks by detecting radiation outside the pipe, enabling precise location without excavation.

image.alt: Pipeline leak detection using isotopic tracer
image.caption: Isotopic tracers enable non-destructive leak detection.

examples.explanation: Stable isotopes like C-13 track nutrient metabolism in humans without radiation exposure.
Isotope ratios in water molecules reveal source, age, and flow patterns of aquifers.

sections.relationship: Isotopes demonstrate that atomic identity (protons) and atomic mass (protons + neutrons) are independent properties, enabling diverse applications from medicine to energy to archaeology.

sections.introduction: Not all isotopes are stable. The ratio of neutrons to protons determines whether an isotope is stable or will undergo radioactive decay.

process_flow.description: Isotopes with balanced neutron-to-proton ratios that do not undergo radioactive decay.
process_flow.mechanism: For light elements (Z < 20), stable isotopes typically have N ≈ Z. For heavier elements, more neutrons are needed (N > Z) to overcome proton-proton repulsion.
process_flow.result: Stable isotopes persist indefinitely in nature and make up most ordinary matter.

image.alt: Chart of stable isotopes showing neutron-proton balance
image.caption: Stable isotopes have balanced neutron-to-proton ratios.

process_flow.description: Isotopes with imbalanced neutron-to-proton ratios that undergo radioactive decay to achieve stability.
process_flow.mechanism: Too many neutrons: beta decay converts neutron to proton. Too many protons: positron emission or electron capture. Very heavy nuclei: alpha decay reduces both protons and neutrons.
process_flow.result: Unstable isotopes decay over time, characterized by their half-life, until reaching a stable configuration.

image.alt: Decay pathways showing unstable to stable transitions
image.caption: Unstable isotopes decay toward stable neutron-proton ratios.

process_flow.description: The time required for half of a radioactive sample to decay.
process_flow.mechanism: Half-life is constant for each isotope, unaffected by temperature, pressure, or chemical state. Ranges from fractions of a second to billions of years.
process_flow.result: Half-life determines practical applications: short half-lives for medical tracers, long half-lives for dating and energy.

image.alt: Graph showing exponential decay over multiple half-lives
image.caption: Half-life is constant for each radioactive isotope.

sections.introduction: Several misconceptions about isotopes are common among students. Clarifying these helps build accurate understanding of atomic structure.

categories.type: Misconception: Isotopes Have Different Chemical Properties
categories.description: Students often think isotopes behave differently in chemical reactions.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Reality: Chemical properties depend on electron configuration, which is determined by proton count. All isotopes of an element have the same number of protons and electrons, so they react identically. Only nuclear properties differ.

examples.explanation: Water made with deuterium (H-2) instead of protium (H-1) has nearly identical chemical properties but different physical properties like density and boiling point.

image.alt: Regular water vs heavy water comparison
image.caption: Isotopes have identical chemical behavior but may differ physically.

categories.description: Students often assume isotopes are inherently radioactive.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Reality: Most naturally occurring isotopes are stable. Radioactive isotopes (radioisotopes) are a subset that have unstable nuclei. Many elements have both stable and radioactive isotopes.

examples.explanation: Carbon-12 and carbon-13 are stable and make up nearly all natural carbon. Only carbon-14 is radioactive, and it comprises only about 1 part per trillion of natural carbon.

image.alt: Abundance comparison of carbon isotopes
image.caption: Most carbon isotopes are stable; only C-14 is radioactive.

categories.type: Misconception: Atomic Mass Equals Mass Number
categories.description: Students often confuse the whole number mass number with the decimal atomic mass on the periodic table.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Reality: Mass number is the count of protons + neutrons in a specific isotope (always a whole number). Atomic mass is the weighted average of all natural isotopes (usually a decimal). They are equal only for elements with one natural isotope.

examples.explanation: Fluorine has only one natural isotope (F-19), so its atomic mass (19.00 amu) equals its mass number. Chlorine has two isotopes, so its atomic mass (35.45 amu) is between Cl-35 and Cl-37.

image.alt: Periodic table entries showing whole vs decimal atomic masses
image.caption: Single-isotope elements have whole number atomic masses; multi-isotope elements have decimals.

summary: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons, resulting in different mass numbers. Isotopes have identical chemical properties but may differ in nuclear stability and physical properties. Natural abundance is the percentage of each isotope found in nature. Atomic mass on the periodic table is a weighted average of all naturally occurring isotopes, calculated as Σ(isotope mass × fractional abundance), which is why it is usually a decimal number. Mass number is a whole number for specific isotopes; atomic mass is a weighted average for natural element samples. Isotopes have diverse applications: medical diagnostics and treatment (Tc-99m, I-131), radiometric dating (C-14, U-Pb), nuclear energy (U-235 fission), and industrial tracers. Stable isotopes have balanced neutron-to-proton ratios; unstable isotopes undergo radioactive decay with characteristic half-lives. Common misconceptions include thinking isotopes have different chemical properties (they don't), that all isotopes are radioactive (most are stable), and confusing mass number with atomic mass (whole number vs. weighted average decimal).
